turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court in Case 22-846, Department of Agriculture versus Kirtz.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: A credit score can determine everything from whether an individual can purchase a home, buy a new car, or start a small business, but lenders who supply the information used to generate credit scores sometimes make mistakes. Sometimes, for example, a lender will tell a credit agency that an individual has defaulted on a loan when he is not. Mistakes like that can impair a consumer’s credit score and make it difficult or impossible for him to obtain a mortgage, auto loan, or other credit. Congress adopted the Fair Credit Reporting Act to address those kinds of mistakes. The Act creates a mechanism for consumers to alert lenders about their reporting errors. It imposes a duty on lenders to correct those errors. And it provides that if a lender willfully or negligently fails to fix its mistakes, a consumer can sue for damages. In this case, Reginald Kirtz secured a loan from the U.S. Department of Agriculture. According to Mr. Kirtz, the agency reported that the loan was passed due long after he had repaid it in full. Mr. Kirtz brought the error to the agency’s attention, but it failed to take any corrective action. So, eventually, Mr. Kirtz sued. In response, the government argued that it was immune from suit. Yes, the government acknowledged a private lender would be liable to a consumer in these circumstances. But, it said, the doctrine of sovereign immunity prevents consumers from suing the government for even willful mistakes. Mr. Kirtz disagreed, arguing that the Fair Credit Reporting Act clearly waives sovereign immunity and places the government on equal footing with private lenders. Who is right about this matters not just to Mr. Kirtz, but to millions of consumers like him who have received loans from the government. It matters too, because lower courts have reached conflicting judgments. Some have held that sovereign immunity bars suits like Mr. Kirtz’s, others have held that the law clearly permits them. We took this case to resolve that conflict in lower court authority, ultimately for reasons set forth in the opinion we filed with the Clerk today, we hold that sovereign immunity is no bar to suit and that the Fair Credit Reporting Act renders the federal government and private lenders alike liable for their errors. The court’s decision is unanimous.
